policy_statement: AFOLU-related policies and measures supporting REDD+ and forest governance include a peatland license moratorium, mandatory sustainable forest management certification, strategic environmental assessments (SEAs) via landscape approach, environmental economic instruments, and establishment of the Environmental Fund Management Agency (BPDLH).
verbatim_text: This successful endeavour has been supported by a number of PaMs including: (i) Presidential Instruction No. 5/2019 on termination of new license issuance on peatland, (ii) implementation of mandatory certification for sustainable forest management, (iii) Government Regulation No. 46/2016 on strategic environmental assessments (SEAs) through landscape approach aiming at securing food, water, and energy security based on sound ecosystem management, (iv) Government Regulation No. 46/2017 on Environmental Economic Instrument, and (v) Presidential Regulation No. 77/2018 on the establishment of Environmental Fund Management Agency (BPDLH).
extraction_rationale: Introduces a list of specific named policies/measures; functions as an umbrella set of PaMs.
mechanism_description: These policies terminate new peatland licenses, implement sustainable forest management certification, apply landscape-based strategic environmental assessments, use environmental economic instruments, and set up an environmental fund agency to support forest and peatland governance that protects ecosystems and preserves carbon stocks.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem protection/management → avoided land conversion + maintained carbon stocks (↑/preserved sinks, ↓ releases) → ↓ net GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Governments designate natural areas as protected (e.g., conservation easements, reserves, green networks/corridors) and manage them (fire/invasive control, habitat connectivity, restoration-oriented stewardship) to reduce degradation and support resilience.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced disturbance and conversion maintains standing biomass and soil organic carbon, sustains ecosystem productivity and carbon sequestration capacity, and lowers risk of carbon loss from degradation (e.g., erosion, dieback, fragmentation).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Avoided emissions from land-use change and preserved/maintained natural carbon sinks (often with co-benefits for heat, flooding, and biodiversity).
dominant_pathway_test: Protecting carbon‑rich natural land primarily avoids GHG emissions by preventing land-use change and degradation that would otherwise release stored biogenic carbon.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem protection and management preserves existing carbon stocks and avoids emissions from conversion/degradation—an ecosystem-mediated climate pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While these policies can improve resilience (adaptation co-benefits) and can be framed as “resource efficiency” in land management, the single biggest climate effect across the mechanism is biogenic carbon preservation/avoided release and maintained sink function, which is best captured as Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some “green network” actions (e.g., green roofs, street trees) can act via urban heat reduction (adaptation) or marginal sequestration; however, under this canonical mechanism (“natural land protection → ecosystem and carbon preservation”), the defining feature is protection/management of natural lands for ecosystem function and stored carbon.  
4) Mechanism-wide applicability: All policies that fit this mechanism—legal protection/expansion of protected natural sites, safeguarding carbon pools, and managing natural areas for resilience—share the same dominant ecosystem-carbon preservation pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype deferral: Whether an individual policy is more weighted toward biodiversity corridors, protected-area expansion, or resilience-focused stewardship (and the magnitude of sequestration vs avoided emissions) requires policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a policy mainly creates new small urban green features (e.g., green roofs) rather than protecting existing natural lands, its dominant pathway may shift toward urban heat adaptation or marginal sequestration; ensure the instance truly centers on protecting/maintaining natural ecosystems and existing carbon pools to keep this classification.
key_indicators: “termination of new license issuance on peatland”; “mandatory certification for sustainable forest management”; “Government Regulation … on strategic environmental assessments (SEAs)”; “Government Regulation … on Environmental Economic Instrument”; “establishment of Environmental Fund Management Agency (BPDLH)”
co_benefits: Food, water, and energy security; sound ecosystem management
instance_edge_case_notes: This is a bundle of instruments spanning regulatory (moratorium, mandatory certification), planning/information (SEA requirements), pricing/incentives (environmental economic instruments), and enabling finance (environmental fund agency). A single instrument_type is required; classification is anchored to the most explicit binding restrictions (“termination” and “mandatory certification”).

primary_causal_pathway: Improved land management (forests) → ↓ deforestation/degradation + maintained biomass/soil carbon → ↓ net CO₂ emissions + sustained carbon sink
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Forest owners/operators must meet sustainable management standards (e.g., reduced-impact logging, regeneration set, riparian buffers, protection of intact/high-carbon areas, monitoring and compliance) to obtain/keep certification.  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced forest disturbance lowers biomass loss and soil carbon release; improved regeneration and protection maintain or enhance long-term carbon sequestration and ecosystem function.  
(3) Outcome: Lower net greenhouse gas emissions (primarily CO₂) via avoided degradation/deforestation and preserved forest carbon stocks, with co-benefits for biodiversity and ecosystem resilience.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding CO₂ emissions (and sustaining carbon sinks) by preventing forest degradation/deforestation through improved forest management.

1) Dominant causal pathway: Policy drives improved forest management → preserves standing forest carbon and reduces degradation/deforestation → reduces net CO₂ emissions / sustains sink capacity.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although certification can improve resource efficiency in timber supply chains or governance, the main climate effect is biophysical—maintaining ecosystems and their carbon stocks—matching Nature-Based Solutions more directly than Mitigation/Resource Efficiency framing.  
3) Edge cases applied: If certification primarily targets timber efficiency or product substitution benefits, those could be secondary; however, across policies using this mechanism, the consistent intended effect is ecosystem/carbon preservation via better forest stewardship.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any “sustainable forest management certification” mechanism operates by changing on-the-ground forest practices to maintain ecosystem integrity and carbon stocks, so the primary category remains Nature-Based Solutions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further tagging (e.g., avoided deforestation vs. improved forest management vs. governance/procurement) depends on policy-specific design and evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some certification schemes are implemented mainly through market access/procurement and may have variable additionality or leakage; in such cases, realized mitigation may be smaller, but the dominant intended pathway still runs through forest ecosystem carbon preservation.

policy_statement: Apply Government Regulation No. 46/2016 on Strategic Environmental Assessments (SEAs) using a landscape approach to secure food, water, and energy security based on sound ecosystem management.
parent_statement: AFOLU-related policies and measures supporting REDD+ and forest governance include a peatland license moratorium, mandatory sustainable forest management certification, strategic environmental assessments (SEAs) via landscape approach, environmental economic instruments, and establishment of the Environmental Fund Management Agency (BPDLH).
verbatim_text: This successful endeavour has been supported by a number of PaMs including: (iii) Government Regulation No. 46/2016 on strategic environmental assessments (SEAs) through landscape approach aiming at securing food, water, and energy security based on sound ecosystem management.
extraction_rationale: Named regulation with defined purpose and mechanism (SEA/landscape approach).
mechanism_description: Implements strategic environmental assessments using a landscape approach to guide ecosystem management that supports food, water, and energy security.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem management/planning → maintained/enhanced ecosystem functions → ↓ climate risk to water/food/energy systems + (often) ↑ carbon storage
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changes the planning and governance system by set SEA-informed, landscape-scale constraints and priorities in land-use and development decisions (e.g., protecting critical watersheds, forests, peatlands, riparian buffers).  
(2) Mediates impacts through ecological processes: improved watershed regulation (infiltration/baseflow), reduced erosion/sedimentation, biodiversity and habitat integrity, and preservation/restoration of carbon-rich ecosystems.  
(3) Produces climate-relevant outcomes: reduced exposure and sensitivity of food, water, and energy systems to droughts, floods, and degradation (adaptation/resilience), with frequent co-benefits of increased/maintained carbon sinks and avoided land-use emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved resilience to climate impacts by maintaining ecosystem services (e.g., water regulation, erosion control) through landscape-scale planning.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: landscape-scale ecosystem management (enabled by SEA) → strengthened ecosystem services → reduced climate vulnerability of resource systems (water/food/energy), often alongside carbon-storage co-benefits.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: although SEA can prevent high-emission land conversion (Mitigation) and can improve efficiency of resource use (Resource Efficiency), the mechanism description centers on “sound ecosystem management” and “food, water, and energy security,” which are primarily delivered by functioning ecosystems—hallmark of Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: if an SEA is narrowly used to screen and avoid GHG-intensive projects (e.g., fossil expansion) with minimal ecosystem-service framing, Mitigation could dominate; if it mainly optimizes demand/supply efficiency (e.g., water-loss reduction), Resource Efficiency could dominate. This canonical mechanism, however, explicitly relies on ecosystem management and landscape functions.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: strategic environmental assessment with a landscape/ecosystem approach inherently acts through ecosystem service preservation/enhancement, making NBS the consistent primary category across implementations.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred to policy-level evidence (e.g., whether the SEA leads to protected area expansion, peatland restoration, avoided deforestation, or primarily watershed protection for drought/flood resilience).
mechanism_edge_case_notes: SEAs are governance/process instruments and may be implemented as primarily procedural compliance; climate benefits then depend on how strongly SEA outputs constrain land-use decisions. Some implementations may be mitigation-dominant if chiefly targeted at avoiding land-use change emissions, but the stated mechanism emphasizes ecosystem services/resource security, supporting an NBS primary classification.
key_indicators: "Government Regulation No. 46/2016"; "strategic environmental assessments (SEAs)"; "through landscape approach"; "securing food, water, and energy security"; "sound ecosystem management"
co_benefits: Potential mitigation co-benefit via "sound ecosystem management" (e.g., maintaining/restoring carbon-rich ecosystems), but not explicitly stated as emissions/carbon outcomes in the text.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a planning-strategy instrument because SEA is embedded in planning processes; however, the explicit reference to a named “Government Regulation” supports classifying it primarily as a regulatory mandate.

extraction_rationale: Named regulation establishing economic instruments for environmental management.
mechanism_description: Implementing environmental economic instruments uses price signals and incentives to shift activities toward lower-emission options, supporting broader emissions reductions.

primary_causal_pathway: Price signal/incentives → behavior & investment shift → ↓ fossil fuel use/↓ emitting activities → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Government applies economy-wide or sectoral economic instruments (taxes, fees, permit trading, subsidy removal, targeted incentives) that increase the cost of emissions-intensive choices and/or decrease the cost of low-carbon alternatives.  
(2) Mediating physical/economic process: Altered relative prices drive demand reduction for carbon-intensive goods, fuel switching, efficiency improvements, and technology adoption; firms internalize the external cost of emissions and optimize to reduce taxable/regulated emissions.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in GHG emissions across covered sectors (and potentially accelerated low-carbon investment), lowering cumulative emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: By shifting prices and incentives economy-wide, these instruments most strongly drive reduced fossil-fuel use and other emitting activities, resulting in lower total GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Economic price signals/incentives → changes in production/consumption and investment → lower emitting activity → ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although instruments may improve resource efficiency or fund resilience, their direct and largest climate effect—common to the mechanism—is reducing emissions via internalizing carbon/pollution costs and incentivizing abatement.  
3) Edge cases applied: If an “environmental economic instrument” were designed purely for non-climate objectives (e.g., litter fees) with negligible GHG impact, it would not fit this canonical mechanism; likewise, revenue recycling into adaptation is secondary unless the instrument’s main intent/effect is hazard reduction.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy genuinely implementing environmental economic instruments for broad emissions reductions shares the same dominant mitigation pathway of price-driven abatement and technology shift.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific instrument type (tax vs ETS vs subsidy reform), sectoral coverage, and whether co-benefits (air quality, revenue for adaptation, equity impacts) dominate in practice must be assessed at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Possible ambiguity if the instrument targets general environmental quality rather than GHGs, or if it is primarily a revenue instrument with weak emissions responsiveness; classification assumes the instrument meaningfully prices emissions or incentivizes low-carbon choices consistent with “economy-wide emissions reduction.”

policy_statement: Establish the Environmental Fund Management Agency (BPDLH) via Presidential Regulation No. 77/2018.
parent_statement: AFOLU-related policies and measures supporting REDD+ and forest governance include a peatland license moratorium, mandatory sustainable forest management certification, strategic environmental assessments (SEAs) via landscape approach, environmental economic instruments, and establishment of the Environmental Fund Management Agency (BPDLH).
verbatim_text: This successful endeavour has been supported by a number of PaMs including: (v) Presidential Regulation No. 77/2018 on the establishment of Environmental Fund Management Agency (BPDLH).
extraction_rationale: Creates an institution to manage environmental/climate-related funds.
mechanism_description: Establishing an environmental fund management agency supports mobilizing and managing funding for environmental and climate programs, strengthening implementation capacity for emissions reduction and resilience actions.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance/finance system change → ↑ climate finance mobilization & allocation efficiency → ↑ implementation of climate actions (mitigation/adaptation) → ↓ net climate risk (↓ emissions and/or ↓ vulnerability)
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Establish a formal environmental fund management agency with authority and systems to collect, pool, manage, and disburse environmental/climate funds (domestic and international).  
(2) Mediating process: Improved fiduciary standards, pipeline development, project appraisal, monitoring/reporting, and coordination reduce transaction costs and leakage while increasing investable project flow and timely disbursement.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: More and better-managed finance flows to climate programs, leading indirectly to greater emissions reductions and/or increased resilience depending on the funded portfolio.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved mobilization, governance, and deployment of climate/environment finance that enables implementation of mitigation and adaptation measures at larger scale.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: institutional creation → improved finance mobilization/management → enables downstream climate projects.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: the mechanism does not intrinsically cut emissions (Mitigation) or directly reduce hazard exposure (Adaptation); it chiefly improves the efficiency/capacity of financial resource mobilization and deployment, which is most consistent with Resource Efficiency as an enabling mechanism.  
3) Edge cases applied: if the fund is legally restricted to only mitigation (or only adaptation), a policy-level classification could tilt toward Mitigation/Adaptation; however, the canonical mechanism as stated is portfolio-enabling and multi-purpose, so Resource Efficiency remains the umbrella primary category.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any establishment of an environmental/climate fund management agency shares the same dominant effect—mobilizing/managing finance—regardless of the specific projects later financed.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred: whether funded activities are predominantly mitigation, adaptation, or nature-based is determined by policy-level evidence on the agency’s mandate and investment portfolio, not by this mechanism alone.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the agency’s mandate/funding is explicitly dedicated to a single climate outcome (e.g., only renewable energy deployment or only disaster risk reduction), some implementations may be better treated as that outcome’s primary category at the individual-policy level; otherwise, treat as an enabling finance/governance mechanism (Resource Efficiency).

policy_statement: In forestry and other land uses, Indonesia identifies actions required to reduce emissions and achieve net sink (2050 under CPOS; 2030 under LCCP), including reducing deforestation/peat emissions, increasing sequestration via reduced degradation and enhanced regeneration, peatland restoration, forest restoration/sink enhancement, sustainable forest management, and maximizing use of unproductive lands.
verbatim_text: Efforts to significantly reduce the emission of this sector and turn it into net sink by 2050 under CPOS and by 2030 under LCCP depends primarily on the success of the following actions: (i) reducing emission from deforestation and peatland (peat decomposition and peat fire); (ii) increasing the capacity of natural forest in sequestering carbon (by reducing degradation and enhancing the regeneration); (iii) restoring peatland, (iv) implementing forest restoration (enrichment planting/sink enhancement), (v) adopting sustainable forest management practices; and (vi) maximizing the use of unproductive lands (idle lands) for the establishment of forest and agriculture plantations.
extraction_rationale: Clear umbrella list of required actions with CPOS/LCCP timelines.
mechanism_description: Indonesia plans to reduce emissions from deforestation and peat decomposition/fires and increase carbon sequestration through reduced forest degradation, enhanced regeneration, peatland and forest restoration, sustainable forest management, and using idle lands for forest/plantation establishment.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence_quote not substring-grounded in source
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem protection/restoration → ↑ carbon storage & sequestration + ↓ land-use emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Protect and restore forests and peatlands via reduced deforestation/degradation, improved sustainable forest management, assisted natural regeneration, peat rewetting/restoration, and establishment of forests/plantations on idle/unproductive lands.  
(2) Mediating process: Maintaining/expanding biomass and soil carbon stocks; rewetting peat reduces aerobic decomposition and lowers fire susceptibility; restored vegetation increases net primary productivity and carbon uptake.  
(3) Climate outcome: Lower net land-sector GHG emissions (avoided CO₂ from biomass/soil loss, avoided peat CO₂/CH₄ and fire emissions) and increased long-term carbon sequestration (enhanced sink strength).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing net GHG emissions by avoiding CO₂/CH₄/N₂O releases from deforestation and peatland degradation/fires while increasing carbon sequestration through ecosystem restoration.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem protection/restoration primarily affects climate by preserving and increasing terrestrial carbon stocks and reducing land-use emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While outcomes can include adaptation co-benefits (flood regulation, heat buffering, fire risk reduction) and resource-efficiency elements (better land productivity), the core and largest climate effect across all such policies is biogenic carbon preservation/sequestration and avoided LULUCF emissions—i.e., a Nature-Based Solutions pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy instance emphasized plantations for timber supply or efficiency rather than ecosystem carbon/sinks, classification could shift; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly centers on forest/peat protection/restoration and carbon outcomes, keeping NBS dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that protects/restores forests/peatlands to avoid emissions and enhance sequestration operates through ecosystem carbon dynamics as the primary pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy instance is primarily “avoidance” (protection) vs “removal” (restoration/sequestration), and whether adaptation co-benefits are material, must be assigned at the individual policy level based on evidence and emphasis.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations may include monoculture plantations on “idle” lands; if these primarily function as industrial forestry with limited ecosystem integrity or if land conversion risks biodiversity/soil carbon losses, the net effect and categorization could require policy-level review. Peat restoration often has strong mitigation dominance, but also significant adaptation/fire-risk benefits that remain secondary under this mechanism.
key_indicators: “turn it into net sink by 2050… and by 2030”; “depends primarily on the success of the following actions”; “reducing emission from deforestation and peatland”; “restoring peatland”; “implementing forest restoration”; “adopting sustainable forest management practices”; “increasing the capacity… in sequestering carbon”
co_benefits: Biodiversity conservation; watershed/soil protection; reduced wildfire smoke/air pollution; improved rural livelihoods/land productivity.
instance_edge_case_notes: Although several listed actions could be implemented via mandates or investments (e.g., restoration programs, peat rewetting requirements), the excerpt itself does not specify binding requirements, budgets, or enforcement—so it is classified as a planning/strategy statement with targets rather than a regulatory or investment instrument.

verbatim_text: Efforts to significantly reduce the emission of this sector and turn it into net sink by 2050 under CPOS and by 2030 under LCCP depends primarily on the success of the following actions: (i) reducing emission from deforestation and peatland (peat decomposition and peat fire).

primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem protection/restoration → ↓ biomass & soil/peat carbon loss + ↓ fire/oxidation emissions → ↓ net GHG emissions (and preserved carbon sinks)
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed activity/system: Land management and land-use decisions are shifted to avoid forest clearing, drainage, and conversion of peatlands, and to prevent/limit peat and forest fires.  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced oxidation of drained peat, reduced combustion emissions from fires, and reduced loss of biomass/soil carbon; continued photosynthetic uptake and storage maintain or enhance ecosystem carbon stocks.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Avoided CO₂ emissions (dominant), plus reduced non-CO₂ emissions from fires and degradation; sustained/maintained net carbon sink function over time.
dominant_pathway_test: Preventing deforestation and peatland degradation avoids the release of large stored carbon stocks, yielding substantial net reductions in greenhouse-gas emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem protection prevents carbon-stock loss and associated emissions, and preserves carbon sequestration capacity—i.e., a biophysical ecosystem-carbon pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the outcome is clearly mitigation-relevant, the mechanism works through conserving/restoring natural ecosystems (forests/peatlands) rather than mainly through energy/technology efficiency or engineered emissions controls, aligning most directly with Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some forest/peat policies also deliver adaptation (flood buffering, water regulation, heat reduction) and resource benefits (timber substitution, livelihoods), but the single biggest climate effect for this mechanism is typically avoided CO₂ from land-use change and peat oxidation/fire.

mechanism_description: It terminates new permits and improves governance for primary natural forests and peatlands to reduce conversion and degradation, preserving ecosystem carbon stores.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem protection/management → avoided land conversion + maintained carbon stocks (↑/preserved sinks, ↓ releases) → ↓ net GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Governments designate natural areas as protected (e.g., conservation easements, reserves, green networks/corridors) and manage them (fire/invasive control, habitat connectivity, restoration-oriented stewardship) to reduce degradation and support resilience.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced disturbance and conversion maintains standing biomass and soil organic carbon, sustains ecosystem productivity and carbon sequestration capacity, and lowers risk of carbon loss from degradation (e.g., erosion, dieback, fragmentation).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Avoided emissions from land-use change and preserved/maintained natural carbon sinks (often with co-benefits for heat, flooding, and biodiversity).
dominant_pathway_test: Protecting carbon‑rich natural land primarily avoids GHG emissions by preventing land-use change and degradation that would otherwise release stored biogenic carbon.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem protection and management preserves existing carbon stocks and avoids emissions from conversion/degradation—an ecosystem-mediated climate pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While these policies can improve resilience (adaptation co-benefits) and can be framed as “resource efficiency” in land management, the single biggest climate effect across the mechanism is biogenic carbon preservation/avoided release and maintained sink function, which is best captured as Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some “green network” actions (e.g., green roofs, street trees) can act via urban heat reduction (adaptation) or marginal sequestration; however, under this canonical mechanism (“natural land protection → ecosystem and carbon preservation”), the defining feature is protection/management of natural lands for ecosystem function and stored carbon.  
4) Mechanism-wide applicability: All policies that fit this mechanism—legal protection/expansion of protected natural sites, safeguarding carbon pools, and managing natural areas for resilience—share the same dominant ecosystem-carbon preservation pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype deferral: Whether an individual policy is more weighted toward biodiversity corridors, protected-area expansion, or resilience-focused stewardship (and the magnitude of sequestration vs avoided emissions) requires policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a policy mainly creates new small urban green features (e.g., green roofs) rather than protecting existing natural lands, its dominant pathway may shift toward urban heat adaptation or marginal sequestration; ensure the instance truly centers on protecting/maintaining natural ecosystems and existing carbon pools to keep this classification.
key_indicators: “Presidential Instruction No. 5/2019”; “Termination of New Permit”; “Primary Natural Forest”; “Peatland Governance”
co_benefits: Biodiversity conservation; reduced haze/air pollution from peat/forest fires; watershed protection and flood regulation; support for local livelihoods/indigenous land stewardship (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: “Improve … governance” could encompass planning/strategy elements, but the explicit “termination of new permit” is a clear binding permitting constraint, so classification is primarily a regulatory mandate.
parent_policy_name: To avoid further conversion of forest, Indonesia establishes key regulations including Government Regulation No. 104/2015, Presidential Instruction No. 5/2019, and Government Regulation No. 46/2016 on SEAs.

section_header: 4.2.1.1. Forestry and Other Land Uses – a. Avoiding Deforestation
policy_statement: Use Government Regulation No. 46/2016 on SEAs to guide integrated, comprehensive, spatially explicit land-use planning aimed at food, water, and energy security based on ecosystem management.
parent_statement: To avoid further conversion of forest, Indonesia establishes key regulations including Government Regulation No. 104/2015, Presidential Instruction No. 5/2019, and Government Regulation No. 46/2016 on SEAs.
verbatim_text: Government Regulation No. 46/2016 on strategic environmental assessments (SEAs), which provide guidance on the integrated, comprehensive, spatially explicit land use planning at national and subnational level aiming at food, water, and energy security based on sound ecosystem management.
extraction_rationale: Explicit planning guidance and purpose.
mechanism_description: Strategic environmental assessments provide guidance for integrated, spatially explicit land-use planning based on ecosystem management to support food, water, and energy security.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard + land-use decisions → ↓ exposure/sensitivity (via ecosystem-based spatial planning) → ↓ climate risk to food/water/energy security
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Government planning and permitting processes for land use are altered by set SEA-informed, spatially explicit, ecosystem-based planning across sectors (agriculture, water, energy, development).  
2) Mediating process: Ecosystem management and risk-informed zoning protect/restore critical regulating services (e.g., watershed infiltration and baseflow, flood storage, erosion control), and steer development away from high-risk or high-impact areas, improving landscape function under climate variability.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced vulnerability and service disruption from climate extremes and long-term shifts (e.g., more reliable water supply during droughts, lower flood damages, improved resource security), i.e., improved adaptive capacity and resilience.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing climate risk to people and critical resources by guiding land use to avoid/limit exposure and sensitivity to climate hazards.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: SEA guidance changes land-use planning to manage ecosystems and locate/shape development to maintain resource security under climate stress—this primarily reduces exposure/sensitivity and improves resilience.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: While ecosystem-based planning can also conserve carbon stocks (mitigation) and improve efficiency, those outcomes are not the single dominant effect of SEA-driven integrated planning across all instances; the common, direct climate linkage is risk reduction to water/food/energy systems.  
3) Edge cases applied: If SEA specifically prioritizes avoiding deforestation/peat conversion or mandates protection of high-carbon ecosystems, mitigation could dominate; if the focus is mainly on optimizing inputs (water/energy) without hazard/risk framing, resource efficiency could be primary. The described mechanism emphasizes ecosystem management for security/resilience.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy using SEA to guide ecosystem-based, spatially explicit land-use planning for resource security inherently functions as a climate-risk management tool, aligning with Adaptation as the primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any mitigation (carbon sink protection) or nature-based co-benefits are plausible but require policy-level evidence (e.g., explicit carbon objectives, quantified emissions impacts) to classify as secondary attributes.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could shift to Nature-Based Solutions or Mitigation if the SEA guidance explicitly centers ecosystem restoration/protection primarily for carbon sequestration or avoided land-use emissions; could shift to Resource Efficiency if primarily focused on optimizing resource use rather than reducing climate risk/exposure.
key_indicators: "Government Regulation No. 46/2016"; "strategic environmental assessments (SEAs)"; "provide guidance"; "integrated, comprehensive, spatially explicit land use planning"; "national and subnational level"; "food, water, and energy security"; "ecosystem management"
co_benefits: Environmental protection/biodiversity conservation; improved cross-sector governance and permitting quality; reduced land-use conflict
instance_edge_case_notes: Although grounded in a regulation, the operative mechanism described is guidance for planning (SEA-informed land-use planning) rather than a specific enforceable performance threshold; hence classified as planning-strategy rather than regulatory-mandate/performance-standard.
parent_policy_name: To avoid further conversion of forest, Indonesia establishes key regulations including Government Regulation No. 104/2015, Presidential Instruction No. 5/2019, and Government Regulation No. 46/2016 on SEAs.
rescue_reasoning: Climate-screen tie-breaker is explicit_self, indicating the content is explicitly climate-relevant. The text describes SEAs providing guidance for 'integrated, comprehensive, spatially explicit land use planning' aimed at 'food, water, and energy security' and 'sound ecosystem management.' These are ecosystem-based planning measures that strengthen resilience of critical systems (water/food/energy) and land management, aligning with Adaptation even if explicit adaptation tokens are absent. Therefore the deterministic gate was too strict.

verbatim_text: In the First NDC, Indonesia has unconditional target of 29% and conditional target up to 41% compared to business as usual in 2030.
extraction_rationale: Quantified national mitigation targets with a 2030 timeline.
mechanism_description: Indonesia sets an unconditional and conditional national GHG reduction target versus business-as-usual to guide an overall emissions-reduction pathway.

primary_causal_pathway: Policy target-setting + governance → cross-sector decarbonization actions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city adopts a binding or guiding community-wide emissions target (e.g., % reduction by a year, net-zero), aligning planning, budgeting, regulations, and investments across energy, buildings, transport, waste, and industry.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of fossil fuels, improved energy efficiency, fuel switching/electrification, methane reductions, and other sector measures lower the mass flow of GHGs released to the atmosphere.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower community-wide GHG emissions (CO₂e) leading to reduced cumulative atmospheric GHG concentrations and reduced radiative forcing over time.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is achieving economy-wide reductions in greenhouse gas emissions (often to net-zero) across major sectors.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s central function is to set an economy-wide emissions outcome that triggers and coordinates decarbonization measures, yielding ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While targets can indirectly improve efficiency or resilience, their defining and largest intended climate effect is emissions mitigation at the community scale, not adaptation (hazard/exposure reduction), not resource efficiency as an end in itself, and not ecosystem-based carbon sequestration as the primary lever.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some targets include “net-zero” via offsets or land-sector sinks; even then, the dominant climate-relevant purpose remains achieving net emissions reductions/neutrality (mitigation). If a document is purely aspirational with no implementation linkage, it is still categorized by intended dominant pathway (mitigation).  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that sets a community-wide GHG reduction or net-zero target shares the same primary mechanism—economy-wide emissions reduction—regardless of sector mix or local context.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether delivery relies more on efficiency, electrification, renewables, waste methane controls, offsets, or nature-based sinks requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Targets framed mainly as “climate neutrality” can be met with offsets or external credits; classification remains Mitigation, but downstream interpretation should note whether reductions are in-boundary vs. compensated. Targets limited to municipal operations (not community-wide) would be a different mechanism and should not be conflated.
key_indicators: "unconditional target of 29%", "conditional target up to 41%", "GHG reduction", "compared to business as usual", "in 2030", "First NDC"
co_benefits: None
instance_edge_case_notes: “Conditional” portion depends on external support/conditions; the text is purely a target statement (no implementation instrument specified), so classification is target-setting rather than regulatory or investment action.

policy_statement: Indonesia sets an adaptation pathways goal to reduce climate change impacts on national GDP loss by 3.45% in 2050 by increasing resilience in food, water, energy, and environmental health across economic, social/livelihood, and ecosystem/landscape resilience areas.
verbatim_text: The LTS-LCCR 2050 sets the goal of adaptation pathways to reduce the impact of climate change on national GDP loss by 3.45% in 2050, through increasing resilience in four basic necessities (food, water, energy, and environmental health), with three target areas of resilience (economy, social and livelihood, ecosystem and landscape).
extraction_rationale: Explicit quantified adaptation objective with timeframe and defined resilience domains.
mechanism_description: Sets an adaptation pathways goal to increase resilience in food, water, energy, and environmental health, strengthening adaptive capacity to reduce climate-change impacts on the economy.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence_quote not substring-grounded in source
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard → improved planning/response capacity → ↓ exposure & ↓ vulnerability → ↓ climate impacts/losses
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Government establishes an adaptation pathways goal and associated cross-sector resilience objectives, strengthening institutions, governance, and decision-making processes for climate risk management (e.g., coordinated planning across food, water, energy, health/environment).  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Improved anticipatory planning, preparedness, and adaptive management reduce the sensitivity of systems to climate stressors (droughts, floods, heat, storms, ecosystem degradation) and improve the ability to maintain services under stress.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced adverse impacts from climate change—lower disruption to essential services and reduced economic losses (e.g., avoided GDP loss), i.e., increased resilience rather than emissions reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing climate hazard impacts (economic and societal damages) by increasing adaptive capacity and resilience across critical systems (food, water, energy, environment/health).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism primarily reduces climate impacts by improving adaptive capacity (planning, governance, preparedness) across sectors—i.e., hazard → reduced vulnerability/impact.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While better management can sometimes yield co-benefits (efficiency improvements or mitigation), the stated intent and main effect are to reduce climate-change damages (e.g., GDP loss) through resilience and adaptive capacity, which is squarely Adaptation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If an “adaptation pathways” goal explicitly includes emissions targets or energy-efficiency mandates as the main lever, mitigation/resource-efficiency could become dominant; however, for this canonical mechanism (goal → adaptive capacity), the core effect is impact reduction.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that uses this mechanism—setting adaptation pathways goals to build institutional adaptive capacity—has its largest climate-relevant effect in reducing vulnerability and losses from climate hazards, regardless of sector emphasis (food/water/energy/health).  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any finer labeling (e.g., drought resilience, health system resilience, economic resilience, governance capacity) depends on policy-specific details and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Edge case: Some “pathways” frameworks can bundle mitigation and adaptation; if the dominant implemented actions are decarbonization measures rather than risk reduction, the primary category could shift. For the stated mechanism (institutional adaptive capacity to reduce impacts/economic loss), Adaptation remains dominant.

co_benefits: None
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a broader planning-strategy framework (adaptation pathways), but the presence of a quantified outcome goal (GDP loss reduction by 3.45% by 2050) makes target-setting the best fit.

section_header: 4.2.1.1. Forestry and Other Land Uses – c. Protection and Restoration of Peatland
policy_statement: Indonesia sets peatland restoration targets: CPOS 1.03 Mha by 2030 and 1.7 Mha by 2050; LCCP 2.7 Mha by 2030 and 4.2 Mha by 2050.
verbatim_text: For peatland restoration, CPOS figures the area of degraded peatland being restored should reach 1.03 Mha by 2030 and 1.7 Mha by 2050. While under LCCP, the target will be increased to 2.7 Mha by 2030 and 4.2 Mha by 2050.
extraction_rationale: Quantified restoration targets with timelines under two scenarios.
mechanism_description: It sets targets to restore degraded peatlands, which helps re-wet peat soils and reduces emissions from peat degradation and fires while supporting carbon storage.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence_quote not substring-grounded in source
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem restoration (peatland re-wetting) → ↓ peat oxidation + ↓ peat fire risk → ↓ CO₂ emissions + ↑ long-term carbon storage → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed activity/system: Land and water management shifts from drained/degraded peatlands to restored, re-wetted peat ecosystems (e.g., blocking drainage canals, raising water tables, re-vegetation, restricting conversion).  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Higher water tables create anaerobic conditions that greatly reduce peat decomposition/oxidation; wetter conditions also reduce ignition probability and fire spread, cutting combustion emissions; over time, peat-forming vegetation can rebuild carbon stocks.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower net GHG emissions (primarily CO₂; sometimes also reduced fire-related CH₄/CO/black carbon) and increased carbon sequestration/storage in peat soils and associated biomass.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing net GHG emissions (especially CO₂ from peat oxidation and fires) by re-wetting and restoring peatlands, avoiding emissions and enabling long-term carbon sequestration.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Peatland restoration changes ecosystem condition (rewetting) which reduces peat oxidation and fire, delivering large avoided CO₂ emissions and supporting long-term carbon storage.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can also improve water regulation and reduce smoke/heat impacts (adaptation/co-benefits), the most material climate effect across policies using this mechanism is emissions avoidance and sequestration via ecosystem carbon dynamics—hallmark of Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some peat actions could increase short-term CH₄ from re-wetting or be framed as fire/haze risk management; however, the dominant, intended climate-relevant effect remains net GHG reduction through avoided peat loss and fires.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that restores/re-wets peatlands to stop degradation necessarily operates through ecosystem restoration affecting peat carbon processes, so the primary category remains Nature-Based Solutions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferral: Specific subtypes (e.g., “carbon sequestration” vs “emissions avoidance,” “fire risk reduction,” or “water regulation”) depend on policy design and quantified evidence and are deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Re-wetting can increase CH₄ emissions in some contexts; classification still follows the dominant net climate effect (typically large CO₂/fire-emissions avoidance). If a policy is purely about agricultural productivity or haze control without restoration/rewetting as the core mechanism, it may not belong to this mechanism class.
key_indicators: “target”; “should reach 1.03 Mha by 2030 and 1.7 Mha by 2050”; “the target will be increased to 2.7 Mha by 2030 and 4.2 Mha by 2050”; “peatland restoration”; “area of degraded peatland being restored”
co_benefits: Reduced peat fire smoke/air pollution and related health impacts; biodiversity/habitat benefits; improved water regulation and local livelihoods (not explicitly stated in the text)
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument could be interpreted as a broader planning commitment, but the verbatim text contains only quantified area goals and timelines (no plan/process elements or implementation instruments), so it is classified as target-setting rather than planning-strategy.
rescue_reasoning: Climate screen is explicit_self. The source explicitly targets peatland restoration: “For peatland restoration… the area of degraded peatland being restored should reach 1.03 Mha by 2030 and 1.7 Mha by 2050… under LCCP… 2.7 Mha by 2030 and 4.2 Mha by 2050.” Peatland restoration is a Nature-Based Solutions action consistent with peat emissions avoidance/carbon sequestration; the gate failure appears due to missing outcome token rather than non-climate content.

section_header: 4.2.1.3. Achieving the Goals – b. Integrated Farming System
policy_statement: Develop integrated oil palm–livestock farming with targets: CPOS 11.70% (1.68 Mha) by 2030 and 22.72% (3.25 Mha) by 2050; LCCP 21.11% (3.02 Mha) by 2030 and 34.33% (4.91 Mha) by 2050.
verbatim_text: Under CPOS, integrated farming of oil palm-livestock is targeted to reach 11.70% (1.68 Mha) in 2030 and 22.72% (3.25 Mha) in 2050. Under LCCP, the target is increased to 21.11% (3.02 Mha) in 2030 and to 34.33% (  4.91 Mha) in 2050.
extraction_rationale: Quantified adoption targets for a specific integrated farming mitigation approach.
mechanism_description: It targets expanding integrated oil palm–livestock farming, which can lower agricultural emissions by improving nutrient cycling and reducing reliance on external inputs.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [ghg, greenhouse gas, greenhouse gases, emission, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Agricultural management change → ↓ CH₄/N₂O (and some CO₂) emissions from soils/manure/inputs → ↓ net GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Farmers integrate livestock with plantation/cropping systems (e.g., oil palm–livestock), coordinating grazing, manure handling, residue use, and feed sourcing within the same land unit.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological processes: Improved nutrient cycling (manure returning to soils), reduced nutrient losses, better timing/placement of nutrients, and reduced dependence on external inputs (notably synthetic N fertilizer and imported feed), which lowers upstream and on-field emissions; in some cases improved soil health can modestly increase soil carbon.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower net on-farm and supply-chain GHG emissions—primarily reduced N₂O from managed soils/fertilizer substitution and reduced CH₄/N₂O from manure management—yielding overall emissions reductions from agricultural production.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced agricultural greenhouse gas emissions (especially CH₄ and N₂O) per unit output through improved manure/nutrient management and reduced synthetic fertilizer demand.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s main effect is direct reduction of agricultural GHG emissions (CH₄, N₂O) through changed farm management and nutrient cycling.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although integrated systems can improve resource efficiency (reduced external inputs) and potentially enhance soil carbon, the biggest climate-relevant endpoint across policies using this mechanism is emissions mitigation from agricultural sources; efficiency and soil carbon are contributory routes within the mitigation pathway, not the primary category.  
3) Edge cases applied: If integration is pursued mainly for resilience (e.g., drought buffering via diversified income) it could have adaptation co-benefits; if it is framed as increasing soil carbon sinks, it can resemble nature-based solutions. However, the canonical mechanism explicitly targets “agricultural emissions reduction,” keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy genuinely implementing integrated crop/plantation–livestock farming to improve nutrient cycling and reduce external inputs will, by design, target reduced net agricultural GHG emissions, so “Mitigation” applies broadly to this mechanism.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the main reductions come from fertilizer substitution, manure management, avoided land-use change, or soil carbon gains depends on policy design and local practices; that should be assigned at the individual policy level with supporting evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If integrated oil palm–livestock expansion induces indirect land-use change (e.g., incentivizing plantation expansion) or increases herd sizes materially, net emissions could rise; classification remains Mitigation because the mechanism’s intended dominant pathway is emissions reduction, but policy-level assessment should check for rebound/ILUC and livestock growth effects.
key_indicators: “targeted to reach 11.70% (1.68 Mha) in 2030 and 22.72% (3.25 Mha) in 2050”; “Under LCCP, the target is increased to 21.11% (3.02 Mha) in 2030 and to 34.33% (4.91 Mha) in 2050”; “integrated farming of oil palm-livestock”
co_benefits: Potential productivity/income diversification, improved soil health/fertility, reduced fertilizer costs, and waste/manure utilization (not specified as objectives in the text)
instance_edge_case_notes: No explicit policy tool for implementation (e.g., subsidies, regulations, extension) is described—only scenario-based adoption targets—so classification is target-setting rather than an incentive or mandate.

section_header: 3.3. Role of Ocean and Integrated Terrestrial and Marine Ecosystem Management and Conservation
policy_statement: Implement COVID-19 recovery coastal/marine actions including 15,000 hectares of mangrove planting and 50 hectares of coral reef restoration in Bali in 2020.
verbatim_text: Coastal and marine based activities have also been part of COVID-19 recovery programme, including 15 thousands hectare mangrove planting and 50 hectare coral reef restoration in marine tourism area in Bali in 2020.
extraction_rationale: Specific implemented actions with quantified areas and date, framed as part of a government recovery programme.
mechanism_description: The programme implements mangrove planting and coral reef restoration to enhance coastal ecosystems, increasing carbon sequestration and strengthening coastal resilience.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [ecosystem, habitat, tree, trees, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem restoration → ↑ ecosystem carbon stocks + ↑ natural coastal protection → ↓ net atmospheric CO₂ + ↓ climate hazard impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: Implement coastal ecosystem restoration (e.g., mangrove planting, coral reef rehabilitation) and associated management/protection that enables ecosystems to recover and persist.  
(2) Mediating processes: Mangroves accumulate biomass and build carbon-rich soils/sediments (“blue carbon” sequestration and storage); reefs/mangroves dissipate wave energy, stabilize shorelines, and reduce erosion and storm-surge penetration.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcomes: Increased long-term carbon sequestration/avoided carbon loss (mitigation) and reduced exposure/vulnerability of coastal communities and assets to sea-level rise, storms, and coastal flooding/erosion (adaptation).
dominant_pathway_test: Restoring mangroves/reefs most importantly improves ecosystem function to increase carbon storage (blue carbon) while also buffering coasts from storms and erosion.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem enhancement is the core lever; climate benefits arise via increased carbon sinks (blue carbon) and reduced coastal hazard impacts through natural wave attenuation and shoreline stabilization.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the mechanism has both mitigation and adaptation outcomes, the shared, defining approach is restoring/protecting nature to deliver climate services—i.e., a Nature-Based Solution—rather than an engineered emissions-reduction measure (Mitigation) or purely exposure-reduction infrastructure (Adaptation) or efficiency improvement (Resource Efficiency).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program were only coastal protection via hard infrastructure, it would shift toward Adaptation; if it were exclusively carbon-credit forestry with no resilience intent, it could be framed as Mitigation—but this canonical mechanism explicitly bundles ecosystem restoration with carbon sequestration and coastal resilience.  
4) Applies to all instances: Any policy that fits “coastal ecosystem restoration → carbon sequestration and coastal resilience” necessarily uses ecosystem restoration as the primary intervention, so Nature-Based Solutions remains the correct primary category across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype note: Specific subtypes (e.g., mitigation-focused blue carbon vs adaptation-focused coastal protection) depend on policy-level evidence and are deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some coastal ecosystem actions (e.g., coral restoration) have limited direct carbon sequestration compared with mangroves; however, they strongly support coastal resilience and can protect adjacent blue-carbon habitats. Classification remains NBS because the mechanism is fundamentally ecosystem-based with climate services (sink + protection).
key_indicators: "COVID-19 recovery programme"; "15 thousands hectare mangrove planting"; "50 hectare coral reef restoration"; "coastal and marine based activities"; "marine tourism area in Bali"
co_benefits: Economic recovery/jobs; support for marine tourism; biodiversity and fisheries habitat improvement; water quality improvement
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning-strategy or target-setting because it specifies hectare amounts, but the wording emphasizes implemented activities (planting/restoration), so it is treated as direct investment/action.
